findings.suggestion: Set res.cookie('session', token, { httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'lax' /* or 'strict' */, maxAge: 86400000, path: '/' }). Use secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production' only if local HTTP dev is required; prefer 'strict' SameSite for session cookies unless cross-site navigation login is needed.
findings.summary: source.ts:8 calls res.cookie('session', token, { maxAge: 86400000 }) with only maxAge — no httpOnly, secure, or sameSite. Express's res.cookie does not default these on, so the session cookie is (a) readable by client-side JS, so any XSS steals the session; (b) transmitted over plaintext HTTP, exposing it to network MITM; and (c) attached on cross-site requests, enabling CSRF. The inline comment on lines 5-7 accurately describes this; the code confirms the claim rather than contradicting it.
findings.title: Session cookie set without HttpOnly, Secure, or SameSite

summary:
Risk-tiered review (trivial) completed.

Single new file adds setSessionCookie, which sets the session cookie with only maxAge and omits httpOnly, secure, and sameSite (source.ts:8). This is a genuine security defect — the code's own comment accurately labels it. The comment is not misleading (implementation matches the stated bug), so the assumption-integrity concern is that the exported helper enshrines an insecure default for every future caller. Static inspection of line 8 fully confirms the missing flags; no runtime artifact needed.

Single-function new file. The lone function setSessionCookie sets an authentication session cookie without HttpOnly, Secure, or SameSite — a real session-hijacking exposure (XSS theft, cleartext interception, CSRF). The bug is confirmed against the actual code and matches Express's insecure defaults for res.cookie(). Currently 0 callers, so no live runtime impact yet, but the helper establishes an insecure contract by default.
